message.content:
PLAYER: "Scrodinger" (multiple accounts).
RATINGS: chess.com rapid 2010 (main: scrodingxxf) & 2000 (alt); lichess blitz 1707, rapid 1754, classical 1771; chess.com blitz best 1612, currently lower; bullet ~1150.
TACTICS/PUZZLES: chess.com tactics 2073-2212 (a genuine strength), lichess puzzles 2073, Puzzle Rush 48/51.
ESTIMATED FIDE OTB: ~1600-1700. Has NOT played FIDE-rated OTB yet.
REPERTOIRE — White: 1.e4 player. vs 1...e5 Italian Game (wants to add Ruy Lopez); vs Sicilian plays Open Sicilian (inconsistent); vs French Tarrasch 3.Nd2; vs Caro-Kann Advance 3.e5.
REPERTOIRE — Black: vs 1.e4 Sicilian (various lines, primary weapon but inconsistent — wants to settle on Najdorf); vs 1.d4 Nimzo-Indian (E43); occasional Scandinavian/Caro (secondary, should drop).
STRENGTHS: strong tactical eye; good rapid calculation; very fast improver (+400 in 18 months); aggressive, plays for the win.
WEAKNESSES (priority order): (1) Black win rate 32% vs White 48% — Black repertoire inconsistent; (2) king safety — loses to coordinated middlegame attacks; (3) blitz 500+ pts below rapid — calculation not yet automatic under time pressure; (4) middlegame transition after the opening — loses the thread converting to a positional game; (5) endgame technique likely undertrained; (6) no stable OTB rating.
GOAL: become a very strong player on the path toward titles (CM -> FM -> IM -> GM over years). Wants a personalized course/resource curriculum.


YOU ARE AN ADVERSARIAL FACT-CHECKER for chess resources. Category: "King Safety, Attack & Defense".

Here are researched resources as JSON:
[
 {
  "name": "The Art of Attack in Chess",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Vladimir Vukovic (rev. John Nunn), Everyman Chess",
  "url": "https://www.everymanchess.com/products/the-art-of-attack-in-chess",
  "cost": "~$24.95 USD print / ~$19.99 ebook",
  "level": "1800-2400",
  "why": "This is THE canonical treatise on attacking the castled king, organized exactly around Scrodinger's named weakness in reverse: it teaches the preconditions of a sound attack (center control, preventing counterplay, weakening the king's cover) and the classic sacrifices (Greek gift, double-bishop, Bxh7). As an aggressive 1.e4 player who plays for the win, he gets a rigorous framework for his existing attacking instinct; and by learning what makes attacks SOUND he will better recognize and refute the coordinated middlegame attacks that currently beat him.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "1 book ~3-4 months"
 },
 {
  "name": "Grandmaster Preparation - Attack & Defence",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Jacob Aagaard, Quality Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/gm-preparation-attack-defence",
  "cost": "€24.99 (~$27 USD) print; ebook via Forward Chess",
  "level": "1800-2500",
  "why": "This single book directly targets BOTH halves of Scrodinger's profile: 8 chapters on attack (feeding his aggressive style and 2010 rapid level) and 4 chapters on defence and prophylaxis (his #2 weakness: losing to coordinated attacks on his king). It is a workbook of themed exercises, which suits his genuine tactical/calculation strength (2073-2212 puzzles) and trains him to calculate defensive resources, not just attacks. Aagaard explicitly says it works from 1800 up, matching his level and his CM->FM->IM title path.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "40-60 hrs over months"
 },
 {
  "name": "How to Defend in Chess: Learn from the World Champions",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Colin Crouch, Gambit Publications",
  "url": "http://www.gambitbooks.com/books/How_to_Defend_in_Chess.html",
  "cost": "~$24.95 USD print / ~$13.99 ebook (Gambit/Kindle)",
  "level": "1700-2200",
  "why": "Defense is the most under-served side of Scrodinger's named #2 weakness (loses to coordinated middlegame attacks on his king). This book is built entirely around the two greatest defenders, Lasker (practical obstacles, counterattack) and Petrosian (prophylaxis, neutralizing attacks before they start). It directly addresses the king-safety losses an aggressive attacker tends to suffer when he is on the receiving end, teaching him to hold rather than collapse.",
  "priority": "Essential",
  "time": "1 book ~2-3 months"
 },
 {
  "name": "Mastering Positional Chess",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Daniel Naroditsky, New In Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/mastering-positional-chess",
  "cost": "~$24.95 USD print / ~$19.99 ebook",
  "level": "1800-2300",
  "why": "Its chapters on Prophylaxis, Defense in Worse Positions, and Building/Breaking Fortresses hit two of Scrodinger's listed weaknesses at once: king safety/defense (#2) and losing the thread in the middlegame transition after the opening (#4). Naroditsky teaches how to root out the SOURCE of an opponent's attack (prophylaxis) rather than only parry threats, which is precisely the skill missing when he gets overrun in the middlegame.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "1 book ~2-3 months"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Art of Attack in Chess (Chessable course, with GM Simon Williams video)",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "Vukovic / Simon Williams, Chessable",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/the-art-of-attack-in-chess/course/24575/",
  "cost": "~$59.99-$99.99 USD (frequent sales)",
  "level": "1800-2300",
  "why": "The interactive MoveTrainer version of the classic above, with ~29 hours of GM Simon Williams video, turns Vukovic's attacking patterns into spaced-repetition drills. This fits Scrodinger's profile better than the book alone for his #3 weakness (calculation not automatic under time pressure: blitz is 500+ pts below rapid) - repetition makes the attacking patterns reflexive so they survive a faster clock, helping close the rapid-to-blitz gap.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "30-50 hrs"
 },
 {
  "name": "Anna's Anatomy of The Attack: Same-Side Castling",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "IM Anna Rudolf, Chessable",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/annas-anatomy-of-the-attack-same-side-castling/course/37539/",
  "cost": "~$49.99-$79.99 USD (frequent sales)",
  "level": "1600-2100",
  "why": "A systematic, step-by-step attacking method for same-side castling positions, which is the most common structure Scrodinger reaches as a 1.e4 Italian/Open-Sicilian player. It teaches a repeatable plan from seizing the initiative to the finish, directly building on his aggressive, play-for-the-win style and giving structure to his middlegame so he stops losing the thread (weakness #4) right when his attack should be starting.",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "20-35 hrs"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Checkmate Patterns Manual",
  "rtype": "Online Course",
  "provider": "Raf Mesotten / CM Geert van der Velde, Chessable",
  "url": "https://www.chessable.com/the-checkmate-patterns-manual/course/17841/",
  "cost": "~$39.99 USD course (sales lower); $39.95 hardcover",
  "level": "1400-2100",
  "why": "1,000 spaced-repetition exercises covering every named mating pattern (Anastasia, Greco, Vukovic, smothered, Boden, Lolli, etc.). For a player whose tactics are already a strength (2073-2212), drilling these to instant recognition converts attacks into wins faster and - just as importantly - lets him SPOT the same mating nets being built against his own king, addressing his king-safety weakness from the pattern-recognition side. Ideal reflex-building for his weak blitz (weakness #3).",
  "priority": "Recommended",
  "time": "15-25 hrs"
 },
 {
  "name": "The Attacking Manual 1 & 2 (2nd editions)",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Jacob Aagaard, Quality Chess",
  "url": "https://www.newinchess.com/attacking-manual-1-2nd-edition",
  "cost": "~€24.99 each (~$27 USD each) print",
  "level": "2000-2500",
  "why": "The most ambitious treatment of attacking dynamics (bringing every piece into the attack, momentum, color schemes, weakest points). Pitched at 2000+, which is exactly Scrodinger's chess.com rapid (2010) ceiling, this is the depth resource for his long-term FM/IM ambitions. ECF Book of the Year - the standard text to push his already-strong attacking play toward a master-level understanding of when a sacrifice truly works.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "60+ hrs (two volumes)"
 },
 {
  "name": "Lectures (Akobian, Finegold, Seirawan) - attack & defense",
  "rtype": "YouTube Channel",
  "provider": "Saint Louis Chess Club",
  "url": "https://www.youtube.com/channel/UCM-ONC2bCHytG2mYtKDmIeA",
  "cost": "Free",
  "level": "1600-2400",
  "why": "Hundreds of free GM lectures, many dedicated to attacking the king and to defensive technique/prophylaxis, illustrated through complete master games. A no-cost, ongoing supplement that reinforces the paid books on both of Scrodinger's attack-and-defense fronts; watching full games also trains the middlegame-transition skill (weakness #4) he loses the thread on.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "ongoing, ad hoc"
 },
 {
  "name": "How to Reassess Your Chess (4th ed.)",
  "rtype": "Book",
  "provider": "Jeremy Silman, Siles Press",
  "url": "https://www.amazon.com/How-Reassess-Your-Chess-Mastery/dp/1890085138",
  "cost": "~$29.95 USD print",
  "level": "1400-2100",
  "why": "Silman frames king safety as one of the core imbalances and gives a decision-making method for the middlegame - directly targeting weakness #4 (losing the thread converting the opening into a positional game). For an attacker who currently relies on tactical instinct, this supplies the positional judgment to know WHEN to attack the king and when to consolidate, supporting the steady rise toward his OTB/title goals.",
  "priority": "Optional",
  "time": "1 book ~2-3 months"
 }
]

For EACH resource: use WebSearch/WebFetch to confirm it genuinely exists, the author/provider is correct, it is currently available, and the price is roughly right. Correct any wrong URL/price/author/level. Set verified=true ONLY if you confirmed it real and accurately described; otherwise verified=false with a note. Default to skepticism: if you cannot confirm a specific course/book really exists, mark verified=false and confidence=low. Drop obvious hallucinations entirely. Also re-check that each "why" is genuinely personalized to Scrodinger and tighten it if weak. Reputable, well-known resources (Silman, Dvoretsky, Vukovic, Naroditsky, Saint Louis CC, established Chessable bestsellers) can be high-confidence. Return the cleaned list.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- contribute:contribute: This skill should be used when the user asks to "contribute to open source", "find an issue to work on", "analyze a repo", "submit a pull request", "review PR feedback", "triage an issue", "sync my fork", "create a release", "clean up contribution branches", "debug CI failures", or mentions open-source contribution workflows. Provides a full-lifecycle open-source contribution system with 12 phases.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

content.text: You've hit your session limit · resets 1:50am (Asia/Calcutta)
